history: The client recently completed an undergraduate degree and is entering the workforce. Within the last two and a half years, they came out to their parents and received negative reactions, leading to increased distance from their family. The client moved to New York for education, which allowed them to explore their identity away from family influence. The relationship with their mother is described as strained, with the mother focusing on practical matters rather than emotional support. There is also tension with a sister who is envious of the client's independence and achievements. The client reports feeling rejected, lonely, and unsupported, and has responded by withdrawing from family interactions.
unlovable_belief: I am bound to be abandoned
intermediate_belief: If I show my true self to my family, I will be rejected and hurt. It's safer to keep my distance and not expect support from them. I must rely on myself because others will not understand or accept me.
intermediate_belief_depression: If my family can't accept who I am, maybe no one truly will, and I'll always feel alone.
coping_strategies: The client withdraws from family interactions and avoids discussing personal matters to protect themselves from further emotional harm. They seek autonomy and self-discovery by maintaining distance and focusing on building a life away from family influence.
situation: Recalling the experience of coming out to parents and the negative aftermath. Preparing for or having interactions with family while maintaining distance.
auto_thought: They'll never really accept me for who I am. No matter what I do, I'll always be a disappointment to them.
behavior: I avoid calling or visiting my parents unless necessary. When I do interact, I keep conversations superficial and don't share personal details. I distance myself emotionally to avoid getting hurt.
